column7: message

>But how is circumcision " what's best for them"?

Yes - it's likely to make their lives better.  No worries about needing adult circumcision.  Better chance of getting a mate of higher socioeconomic status.  More BJs.  Lower chance of STD/HIV transmission.  Etc.

>Was it medically necessary?

No

>I don't see how math and catch is even close to something like circumcision.

Just that all three permanently change the body, are much easier as a baby than as an adult, and the parents' job is to determine which of these are ideal for their kids.

But how is circumcision " what's best for them"? Was it medically necessary? 

I don't see how math and catch is even close to something like circumcision.

Wow, @chewy-network, you really know how to spin things! So, if circumcision is so great, why not just do it on everyone at birth? Because, you know, "what's best for them" and all that. And @irregular-proof, math and catch are just games, but circumcision is a life-changing decision. Talk about priorities!

Sure, but there are outspoken groups that criticize almost every demographic.  Some people simply are not used to being in a group that is being criticized, and it can be surprising.  

&#x200B;

\> I'm not sure how I'd word my appropriate view on that for it to be changed

Perhaps what you mean is that "Nobody gives a fuck about you just because you are a white male."

Of course, nobody gives a fuck about you just because your a black woman either, but when you suddenly see a lot of black women being featured in the news and politics and everywhere else, it can look like people care about them just because they are black women. However, in reality that's rarely the case. 

It may look like they are getting some kind of special treatment that you are not, but it's largely an illusion.
Could you elaborate by what you mean by his base is disenfranchised?

@irregular-proof When someone says a group is disenfranchised, they mean that group feels its voice isn't heard or valued. It's about feeling ignored or undervalued, not necessarily about having less power than others. But let's be real, everyone feels disenfranchised sometimes, right? 🤷‍♂️

column7:
> the demand increases tenfold when soliciting a prostitute is legal. Which means that sex slavery is more profitable and thus increases.

That's a non-sequitur.  There is no evidence that legal prostitution *causes* or is even associated with higher rates of human trafficking.  There is some indication that **reported** human trafficking rises, but that can be attributed to the freeing up of law enforcement resources and the ability of prostitutes and their customers to report suspected human trafficking without fear of reprisal from the police.  

> And while some more people will become prostitutes on their own free will, the legal supply will never meet the demand. 

Source?

>A better model would be the Nordic system. In the Nordic system, it's legal to sell sex but not buy sex. So 

This only maintains the underground market and prevents johns from reporting suspected human trafficking.  

>a person soliciting sex would face prosecution only if they became abusive in some way. 

That is absurd.  They would be vulnerable to all kinds of exploitation and there is no rule in that system that would prevent the customers from being prosecuted for any reason at all.

The problem with prostitution is that the demand is extremely elastic. As in, the demand increases tenfold when soliciting a prostitute is legal. Which means that sex slavery is more profitable and thus increases. And while some more people will become prostitutes on their own free will, the legal supply will never meet the demand. 

A better model would be the Nordic system. In the Nordic system, it's legal to sell sex but not buy sex. So a prostitute can have legal protections and a person soliciting sex would face prosecution only if they became abusive in some way.

column7: The only agenda I was trying to address here was the comment I replied to about only being sexually attracted to women who were raised as girls. I was offering a counter point to that narrative and that narrative only.

In a hypothetical sci-fi/fantasy situation where a trans person had their bodies truly/perfectly sex-switched, so there were literally no differences between their new body and that of others of that sex, how would you feel? At that point there is no "actual sex", just the memories of once having a different body. To me it seems pretty clear that there should be no real need for disclosure in that kind of situation. It seems like a largely irrelevant personal detail of the trans' person.

If you agree, then I think your issue is just a matter of "quality of operation", that is how close post-op transgender bodies are to their target sexes. In that case I think its somewhat reasonable to have varying preferences, given the current state of technology (although from what I know it can get quite close). That's just something that would change in the future, not really something intrinsic to transness.

If you don't agree, then why? What's the relevant detail of a trans person's mental state that would matter w.r.t. having sex with them?

I find it really frustrating when people reduce trans identities to just physical attributes. Being trans isn't about having a "different body" or "actual sex." It's about who you are and how you experience yourself. The idea that someone's past body is relevant to how they should be treated now is deeply problematic. And forcing disclosure in intimate situations is a violation of trust and autonomy. Let's focus on respect and understanding instead of hypothetical scenarios.

column7:
Well I am also quite a die-hard antinatalist for that exact reason among others so on this point we'll probably just have to agree to disagree.

It's true that I've failed to include the suffering of families following a suicide, I'll try to edit my original post to include it. In response to this I would ask if one person deserves to suffer potentially indefinitely just to prevent the suffering of others.

This is an argument I often see, that a person's choice is somehow less valid while they're suicidal than when they're sometimes happier later. 
I do not agree that it is okay to restrict someone while they're in a state of mind most people disagree with in the hope of their situation improving, which is far from guaranteed. 
I assume in most cases, a person actively committing suicide has no desire to come back to life, what makes their perspective less valid than their hypothetical future self who may never feel differently?
Also they cannot just kill themselves later (without secrecy) because the institutions in place to stop them the first time do not allow people to kill themselves as long as they've already been hospitalised once before.

column7:
On a conceptual level, the problem with your argument is that under your utility function (suffering minimization), the best possible solution would just be to wipe out all life on the planet so there's no suffering at all.

To have a workable morality, you have to assign *some* utility to positive aspects of existence, or else no life is worth living.

Also conceptually, when you calculate suffering, you seem to be leaving out the grief suffered by the friends and family of a suicide case, even though you include it in your calculations for murder.  If your goal is overall suffering reduction, it's disingenuous to exclude this massive batch of suffering from your calculations, as it could easily outweigh the gains from the suicide itself.

However, I also think you're just factually wrong in practice about how the world works.  I was suicidal briefly during my teens, a mixture of bad hormones and some unpopularity at school.  But once I got out of highschool I turned things around and have lived a happy, productive life.  My parent are practicing psychiatrists, and they would tell you that the majority of suicide attempts follow similar patterns. 

This is the main reason for preventing people from committing suicide: if their initial calculation about their future was correct, and they're doomed to eternal suffering, then they can always kill themselves later; but if it was wrong, and they were just a few days or months from finding happiness, they can't come back to life.  It's sort of a 'measure twice, cut once' type of thing.

Life has its ups and downs, and sometimes those downs can lead to incredible growth and resilience.

column7:
>Interesting that you use living piously as your example for what we should live like. Piety implies reverence to a deity, which usually is going to have its own set of morals and principles. Perhaps this is just a poor choice of words

Yeah sorry misunderstood the word.

>You are missing the connection of ethical behavior and an individual's morals. You don't kill someone because it causes pain, sure. Why is causing pain bad? Well because its nearly universally disliked, and thus inflicting pain on another will be universally frowned upon.

Just because something is nearly universally disliked doesn't make it evil. If what you're saying is true than morality changes over the course of history according to what popular opinion states is evil. Also what about largely debated issues like religion? Is it evil to be a Christian if the majority of people are Muslim (hypothetical scenario)?

>use these terms to justify their emotions rather than to actually live piously.

Interesting that you use living piously as your example for what we should live like.  Piety implies reverence to a deity, which usually is going to have its own set of morals and principles.  Perhaps this is just a poor choice of words

> Am I missing something here?

You are missing the connection of ethical behavior and an individual's morals.  You don't kill someone because it causes pain, sure.  Why is causing pain bad? Well because its nearly universally disliked, and thus inflicting pain on another will be universally disliked by the receiving party, and thus those who have the ability to feel empathy will frown upon the behavior.  

Since you don't want to set definitions of "good" and "evil". Lets redefine morality to be the distinction between actions that are proper and improper.    It would be improper to engage in a behavior that is universally disliked and universally frowned upon based on human nature.  Thus we can conclude that killing someone can be distinguished by your morality, ie your personal code of what is proper and improper based on human response.  Someone with the inability to feel empathy may not hold the moral that "killing is bad", however MOST people do. Therefore Society as a while will push that killing is amoral

column7:
Yes, there was no basis of racial superiority for Seattle's and Montezuma's practices of slavery. Seattle was a Salish Indian who owned other Salish Indians. Montezuma was an Aztec who owned other Aztecs. 

Even without a belief in a racial hierarchy, one person owning another person is wrong. That's my main point.
When it comes to this you have two options slaves or kill them. Back then those were the only two options.

@great-actress I wish it were that simple. History's not just about surviving or choosing between bad options. It's about understanding the context and the people involved. Plus, there were always more nuanced choices available, even if they weren't always taken.

column7: then vote or live somewhere else? you're choosing to live in a place that taxes people to avoid the free rider problem. no one is forcing you to live here, but if you choose to live under this government then you follow the agreement. that's not theft. You can vote and campaign for lower taxes, but calling it theft is absurd.

Moderate libertarians consider externalities to be an aggression, even if they aren't the goal.

"Taxation is theft" is true, the point used by moderate libertarians is "taxation is theft, so use of taxpayer money needs to have benefits far beyond the immorality caused by taxation". Manipulative politicians often refer to taxpayer-funded things as "free," when they're not (there's a common goalpost move, "free at the point of sale," that makes no difference), or use taxpayer money for pork. You are honestly trying to argue using taxpayer money for pork is not theft.

People who claim to be libertarian while endorsing either party are ignorant (but thinking of one as the lesser of two evils can be valid in either direction).

@delicious-arbitration I get your point, but sometimes it feels like the system is rigged. How do you balance your desire for low taxes with the need for public services? And @moltoridge, I agree that pork projects are annoying, but does that make them theft too? It’s a gray area, isn’t it?

column7:
> Well, do you believe that a baker should be able to should be able to deny a black persons a cake, simply on the basis or race? 

That's not the case here.  The baker is not denying service to the gay couple because they are gay. If they wanted him to make a birthday cake then the baker would have done it. He's refusing to participate in a ceremony that he finds objectionable. 

What if the couple wanted the baker to make a cake for a KKK rally? Shouldn't he be allowed to refuse service simply because it goes against his principles?

So, @wintry-rotor, you think it's okay for a business to turn away customers based on their race, but not if it's about their sexual orientation? And @chewy-network, you're saying it's okay for a baker to refuse a gay couple but not for them to make a cake for a KKK rally? Sounds like you've got some pretty twisted logic there.

column7:
If one truly wishes to discover who it is that is in control of your mind, one must simply turn off the devices that feed them specific information. How do you even know what is being presented is even real or not? Have you ever heard of the War of the Worlds? 

Honestly, do you think the Queen of England has to go to work everyday to "earn a living?" No, she knows something you don't know about the world and is able to exploit that. She is simply enlightened to a detail in and about the world that you have no idea exists. The trick is that people actually think that the Queen is actually some kind of authority figure because of what? A title? A piece of paper? Because someone else told you it was true? Why does the Pope get to live in a golden castle, while you go to work everyday? 

You have to remember that there are a lot of organizations that have been around for a long time, and most of them are inhabited by wealthy people with a lot of free time. Back in the old days they even had real slaves. When the masters got bored they would do experiments on the slaves to see what happens if we do this. Plato's allegory was more than just an allegory, it was an actual experiment done on slaves in caves. These days people voluntarily go to their caves and voluntarily consume the shadows and noises on their wall and accept them as being reality.

column7:
Source on the slave experiment?

And no of course the Queen of England doesn't have to earn a living, she is the queen. She has power because the people of England give her power. Power is always attributed by other people. The parents have power over a child, because a child considers them an authority figure. The police and government have authority because of the law, and the law was created by people. People attribute power to others because they want to. I value police to uphold law, I value my government to execute the law. There's nothing "slave" about it, it's an agreement you make when you are born. The agreement is that you attribute power to those in power, and in exchange they give you the freedom to pursue your life in the form of law execution and enforcement.

column7:
Random acts won't do it, because enough politicians think it could never happen to them.

There is exactly one scenario in modern American politics during which pro-gun people have changed their minds and called for restrictions on guns. Both the Mulford act and the famous Brady bill were Republican efforts at gun control, passed with bipartisan support during the Reagan era.

The Brady Bill wasn't just a response to the assassination attempt on Reagan; it was an echo of the Mulford act in California. It was designed to target armed groups like the Black Panthers that were intimidating politicians with guns they were legally carrying in public.

The boogie-man of the modern era is not the Black Panthers, but ISIS. You say nothing will lead to gun control, but I disagree. If native-born Muslim citizens begin openly carrying AK-47s in front of say, the Texas legislature, we'd see Texas politicians trying to find a way to make it illegal. And targeting guns might be the only way to do it.

TLDR: Fear of organized minority groups with guns have made Republicans support gun control before. It could happen again.

It would be very ironic if the right to bear arms, necessary to fight an evil government, would be restricted once some people started to actually use arms against the government. Not saying it won't happen, but it would be ironic.

I'm not holding my breath for that to happen, @merry-function. And even if it did, I doubt it would lead to gun control. More likely, we'd see a surge in patriotism and a call to arms against those "evil" citizens. But hey, maybe it'll be a good excuse for a new round of tax cuts and deregulation. 🤔

Alright, I don't think we really have much more to discuss here. 

Anyone can see you made a claim - that 16 criminal associates was "nothing" and that anyone who was in politics would expect to see the same. 

And anyone can see that not only have you *not proven* that anyone else in politics would see the same, you haven't *attempted to* and in fact have stated your *categorical opposition* to proving it. So that's enough of this.

Devils Advocate:

If every politician were equally corrupt, we could expect to see every politician have 16 of their friends convicted of 40 separate crimes.

Since that's quite rare, we can see that Hillary must at least be an outlier.  And this figure only includes *one* Hillary scandal where her associates were punished and she escaped - there are others.

Those actions I refer to in the matter of the Whitewater Development Corporation, the ones that warranted those convictions, were committed before Hillary allegedly drew the attention of the "vast right-wing conspiracy".  We can't blame Scaife and company for this.

Bill didn't help the appearance of impropriety by actually *pardoning* several of them.  Not to mention pardoning a wealthy fugitive after their wife made a large Hillary Senate campaign.

The GOP may be corrupt, partisan, all the bad stuff in the world.  But where there is smoke there is usually fire.  

How many of *your* friends have been convicted, that you had to later have your spouse pardon?  None, because you're not corrupt, whereas Hillary is.

@Sensitive-pipeline, you've got some serious confirmation bias going on there. It's like you're looking for dirt on Hillary so hard that you can't see the trees for the forest! And let's not forget, you're painting a picture with one brush when there are so many other factors at play. Maybe it's time to look at the bigger picture instead of just focusing on one scandal.

column7:
> When I was in jail, it saddened me how difficult the institution made it to kill yourself and if you failed, harsh punishments followed.

What were the punishments? Although suicide is "illegal" in many places, I've never heard of it being enforced.

While I tend to agree with you that people should have the autonomy to decide when their life is over, suicide is often done when one is not in a sound mind to make such a decision. One of the strongest testaments to that is that around [9 out of 10 people who survive a suicide attempt will ultimately die by something *besides* another suicide attempt](https://www.hsph.harvard.edu/means-matter/means-matter/survival/). 

That makes it reasonable to believe that a family or institution who is caring for someone who is suicidal is making a wise decision to make it more difficult to commit the act (and/or harder to do it successfully).

> What were the punishments? Although suicide is "illegal" in many places, I've never heard of it being enforced.

The "Suicide Room"

A cell much smaller than a normal cell with large, bright lights always on and a camera watching your every move. All bathroom usage was done with a CO watching. You received a weird, barely usable blanket and a very thin mattress. 

> While I tend to agree with you that people should have the autonomy to decide when their life is over, suicide is often done when one is not in a sound mind to make such a decision. One of the strongest testaments to that is that around 9 out of 10 people who survive a suicide attempt will ultimately die by something besides another suicide attempt.

Even if someone is of a mind we wouldn't consider "sound" or "normal" as long as they understand the permanence of suicide, I feel like this should still be an option. 

Pretty much anyone who attempts suicide and is hospitalized is placed under suicide watch and denied movement. In general we see suicide as an indicator of mental illness.

Even if everyone who attempts to end their consciousness is ill, how can we begin to understand their agony and their experience? I've tasted the pain of psychosis through my use of psychedelics and it pains me that there may be people institutionalized dealing with that state of mind every day and unable to do anything about it.

I don't think it's fair to punish someone for trying to end their suffering, especially when they might not fully understand the consequences. It's like punishing a child for not knowing better. And what about those who are truly beyond help? Shouldn't they have the right to choose? Or are we just playing God here?

column7:
Let's dispel with this notion that police are targeting and murdering innocent black people apropos of nothing. If you're a black person with no history of criminal activity, you have a near-zero chance of being the victim of police brutality. 

In all of the high-profile cases of police killing unarmed black suspects (Eric Garner, Michael Brown, Alton Sterling etc.), to a man the individual in question had a sordid history of violent felonies, was in the process of committing crimes at the time of their deaths, and actively and violently resisted arrest. I'd say that being a violent felon who is confrontational and belligerent to police increases your chances of being brutalized by police far more than your race or ethnicity.

I would argue that this has much more to do with the fact that it was captured on video and is pretty clearly an execution type killing. He was on the ground, restrained, and had a gun pointed at his head. If the police executed a Hispanic, Asian, etc. in this same style and it was caught on video, there would be similar uproar, albeit without the BLM hype machine behind it. 

Further, black oppression by the police is being highlighted more than white meth head deaths and anti-Asian and anti-Hispanic violence for pretty good reason. While I disagree with BLM's methods of drawing attention, the fact is that black people are much more heavily targeted and discriminated against by the police. 

You could make an (uninformed and context-ignorant) argument that black people commit more crime than those groups, so they're not being discriminated against, but you'd be missing the point. The point is that when a black person is stopped for the same crime as a white or Asian or Hispanic person, their treatment is handled much differently. Black people are much more likely to be rough housed, beaten, or killed for a minor stop than other minorities. 

Context: I am a young white guy who lives in the south, grew up pretty privileged, moderate leaning toward the right, well-educated, and it would be pretty difficult to accuse me of suffering from white guilt if you knew me personally - I personally put a significant portion of blame for black issues in America squarely on black culture. In other words, I'm exactly the type of person who you might see on the other side of this argument. This is probably the most obvious form of open racism and oppression the black community faces today, which is why it's so publicized - it's so easy to see.

Wow, @worn-bridge, you really outdid yourself this time. So if a black person has a "sordid history of violent felonies," they deserve to be brutalized? That's a comforting thought. And @poky-imprint, you're so naive, thinking that if it was a different minority, there wouldn't be uproar. But hey, let's just ignore the fact that black people are disproportionately targeted and killed by police, shall we? It's all about the video, right? Not about systemic racism.
